blocks.content: He worked in the unhurried way he always worked when there was no one to serve yet — with the particular care that people bring to things when only they will witness it. He cleaned the steam wand. He warmed the cups. He checked the arrangement of the small table at the back of the room, which was separate from the main tables, set apart by the slight change in light where the back hallway began. He had put a candle there. Nothing elaborate. Just a candle.
He paused, standing in the center of the floor, and looked at what he had made of the place.

He had been running The Copper Mug for nineteen years. He had watched a great many things happen in it — people meeting and separating, decisions being made over coffee that would affect the rest of lives, grief being held quietly at corner tables, love announced and then carefully taken back. A coffee shop was, in his experience, a place where people said the things they had been gathering the courage to say. There was something about the particular intimacy of it — the warmth, the noise that gave you privacy in its cover, the sense that you were not entirely alone even when you were speaking to one person.

He had known, since Ethan had first brought Cassandra through the door, that this particular story was going to become one of the ones that mattered. He was not a man who inserted himself into such things. But he was also not a man who, when he had the means to make something beautiful available to two people who deserved it, would decline to do so on the grounds of neutrality.
He heard footsteps on the sidewalk outside. Then the knock.
